name: Medication documentation
description: MAR charts, medication reviews, GP correspondence
current_process: Medication documentation in care homes revolves around the Medication Administration Record (MAR) chart — a paper or digital document listing every medication prescribed to a resident, including drug name, dose, frequency, route, and administration times. A new MAR chart is produced each month, typically generated by the dispensing pharmacy. At each medication round (usually 4 per day: morning, lunchtime, teatime, bedtime), a trained staff member administers medications and signs the MAR chart immediately after the resident takes each dose. Specific codes are used for refusals, omissions, hospital stays, or self-administration. PRN (as-needed) medications require separate documentation including reason for administration, dose, time, and effectiveness review. Monthly medication audits cross-check MAR charts against prescriptions, stock levels, and GP instructions. When medications change — following a GP review, hospital discharge, or pharmacy update — the care home must reconcile the new prescription with the existing MAR, update care plans, and often correspond with the GP to clarify or confirm changes. Controlled drugs require additional dual-signature registers. Paper MAR charts must be stored for a minimum of 8 years.
time_spent: Estimated 10–15 hours per week across the care team for a 40–60 bed home. Each medication round takes 1.5–3 hours depending on resident numbers and complexity (4 rounds per day). MAR chart documentation itself (signing, coding, PRN records) accounts for roughly 15–20 minutes per round per staff member. Monthly medication audits take 3–5 hours. GP correspondence regarding prescription changes, reviews, and queries adds 2–4 hours per week for the manager or senior carer. Medication error investigations and follow-up reporting add further time. Agencies that switched to digital eMAR systems report 61% less time spent on medication audits and 75% reduction in audit time specifically.
ai_solution: AI can significantly reduce the documentation burden while improving medication safety: (1) Electronic MAR (eMAR) systems with AI-powered alerts that flag missed doses, potential drug interactions, allergies, and dosing errors in real time. (2) Automated medication reconciliation — when a prescription changes (e.g., post-hospital discharge), AI cross-references the new medication list against the existing MAR and highlights discrepancies for staff review. (3) NLP-powered GP correspondence: AI drafts letters to GPs requesting medication reviews, querying prescription discrepancies, or reporting medication-related incidents, using structured templates pre-populated with resident data. (4) Automated monthly audit reports — AI analyses MAR data to produce audit-ready summaries of compliance rates, missed doses, PRN usage patterns, and stock discrepancies. (5) Predictive analytics to identify residents at higher risk of medication errors based on polypharmacy levels, cognitive status, and historical patterns. CareLineLive and CareCallAI both offer eMAR systems with built-in compliance checks in the UK market.
buyer_language: "We've got four medication rounds a day, every single dose has to be signed for, and if something changes with a prescription we're on the phone to the GP for hours trying to sort it out. The monthly audit alone takes the best part of a day. One missed signature and CQC are all over it."
outcome_statement: Medication audits completed in minutes instead of hours, prescription changes reconciled automatically, and real-time alerts that catch errors before they reach the resident — reducing medication incidents and freeing up clinical time.
regulatory_alignment: Maps directly to CQC Quality Statement: 'Medicines optimisation' under the Safe key question. CQC Regulation 12 (Safe care and treatment) requires that medicines are supplied in sufficient quantities, managed safely, and administered accurately in accordance with prescriber instructions. CQC has published specific guidance on medicines administration records in adult social care, stating MAR charts 'must be filled out immediately by the carer after medication has been administered.' Also aligns with 'Learning culture' (Safe) for medication error reporting and learning, and 'Monitoring and improving outcomes' (Effective).
existing_tech_gap: Several UK platforms offer eMAR functionality — CareLineLive, Nourish (via pharmacy integration), Person Centred Software, and ATLAS eMAR. These digitise the signing process and provide basic dashboards, but most still require manual data entry for PRN protocols, medication changes, and GP correspondence. None of the mainstream platforms use AI to auto-reconcile prescription changes from discharge summaries, generate GP correspondence, or provide predictive analytics on medication risk. AI adds unique value through intelligent reconciliation, automated correspondence drafting, and pattern-based risk prediction that goes beyond simple alerting.
implementation_complexity: Requires integration — eMAR systems need connectivity with dispensing pharmacies (for automated MAR generation), GP systems (for prescription updates), and the care home's existing care planning software. Digital signature capture requires tablet or mobile hardware at point of care. Pharmacy partnerships are essential for seamless MAR generation. Controlled drug register integration adds additional complexity.
proof_of_concept_availability: Partial. CareLineLive, ATLAS eMAR, and Person Centred Software all offer working eMAR solutions in the UK market with basic compliance checks. CareCallAI offers AI-powered clinical assessments. The 61% reduction in medication audit time and 75% reduction in audit workload are published metrics from UK care providers who have adopted digital eMAR. However, no UK platform currently offers full AI-powered medication reconciliation from discharge documents or AI-generated GP correspondence as a turnkey product.
